commenters.name: Christof Donat
commenters.commentText: That's not how senior devs talk. More like managers imagine, senior devs talk. Senior devs know, that there are different tools for different jobs, and there's no such thing as a "wrong library".

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: 1. Ok. I have no exact data how many troll on Reddit.2. Yes. Sorry for confusion, US - USA.3. Agree. It would be great to create a definition of senior, but its a topic for another holy war :) So let's stop here :)

commenters.name: Len Jaffe
commenters.commentText: The difference between developer's idea of "can be changed in the future"  and business' idea of it are that the developer values it more than the business values it. In fact, the business typically only values "solves the problem now". Maintainability is clawed back one enhancement crisis at a time

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: Len Jaffe good catch.

commenters.name: Oleksandr Don
commenters.commentText: IMO "Can be changed in the future" leads to accumulating "Architectural debt", rarely addressed in the future until major issue emerges. Chicken or egg situation, has little impact on business unless risk can be estimated and proven quantitatively.

commenters.name: Oleksandr Don
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov I will read through the suggested methodology. Thanks. Adapter (DB gateway) was in place so the problem was more or less contained.

commenters.name: Maryna Tochkova
commenters.commentText: But the time for change can be from 15 minutes up to months in case code duplicates are used everywhere etc..

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: Maryna Tochkova right, thank you for article, I read it already. We have a transition now. AI is writing code. His is the reality. We both saw tons of such code. Good it or bad? From human perspective definitely bad. Why? Because it’s repetitive, not SOLID, hardly understood. Etc.From businesses per

commenters.name: Paul Hammond
commenters.commentText: If one of those people is using Angular, one of them is definitely wrong.

commenters.name: William Alexander
commenters.commentText: Paul Hammond my spidey-senses tingle when otherwise intelligent engineers talk nonsense ^^

commenters.name: Oleksandr Koreniak
commenters.commentText: AI-generated code is simply working code that works and is worth as much as the AI ​​model that generated it. It's simply another code producer in the code supply chain. An experienced engineer knows where and how to use it.

commenters.name: Yurii Kinakh
commenters.commentText: Been thinking this way for a while. Once you stop treating code like art and start treating it like a tool that has to work and stay flexible, most quality debates dissolve.What's tricky tho is the cultural side. Some senior devs feel like their craft is being devaluated when you adopt this mindset.

commenters.name: Yurii Kinakh
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov ha, yeah the "code is art" crowd can get passionate about it. Appreciate the post, it needed to be said.

commenters.name: Sanyam Arya
commenters.commentText: It shouldn't just run, 𝗶𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲. Design principles are exactly what make that "𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗿" requirement actually possible.

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: Sanyam Arya didn’t I stated that?

commenters.name: Yitzhak Kornbluth
commenters.commentText: Yitzhak Kornbluth As for AI-written code that can only be maintained by AI: Even assuming that the AI can in fact reliably maintain it (something that quite a few engineers say they've seen not happening), that locks you in to AI, which could be a problem if the venture capital dries up and the AI c

commenters.name: Mukesh A.
commenters.commentText: Spot on! I’ve seen people doing refactoring to use new tools just because they are hyped to solve the problem better and faster. Conveniently ignoring man hours to get it done and failing to analyse what these changes will add to overall business value and propositions- the things really matter.

commenters.name: Andre Prellwitz
commenters.commentText: Software has this quality where it seems to work for the moment, but fails catastrophically without warning, given the right conditions.  That failure can include function, performance, security, or even legal.  Does this actually work, is it fast enough to compete, is it compatible with our securit

commenters.name: Andre Prellwitz
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov Maybe it's a language or culture effect, but saying something "works" means something entirely different than what you postulated.Of course if AI code passes security scans, meets intuitive performance goals, considers licensing, handles edge cases, etc., there's no issue with it.

commenters.name: Iharob Al Asimi
commenters.commentText: AI code can certainly be modified later by AI. But i increasingly becomes a mess that eventually stops working. I am conducting an experiment rigth now. There is potential, that eventually it will actually be able to do what engineers do. But not yet.

commenters.name: Aleksandr Ishchenko
commenters.commentText: Arguing at the coffee machine is often a sign of juniority. And when they understand your idea about the fact that code is not a holy grail but rather a business instrument, we call them "software developers". And when we can explain this idea to other developers, we call them "seniors."

commenters.name: Lowie Rojas
commenters.commentText: Seniors would never use such language. Maybe you could hear it from juniors, and it is still a maybe.A real senior would try to deal with this using constructive comments.  "Have you considered a different approach, maybe using this other library? Why would you prefer this one instead of the other?"

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: Lowie Rojas ohh. Heav you heard holy wars what’s better .NET or Java?

commenters.name: Yitzhak Kornbluth
commenters.commentText: I have to disagree with your bolded statement.  You named two criteria.1. It solves the problem now.2. It can be changed when requirements shift.  Presumably you mean "easily changed".I think you need to add:3. It is relatively easy to maintain.What makes code maintainable?  There are quite a few fa

commenters.name: Yannick L.
commenters.commentText: Hi Peter OvchynnikovI agree with your point.Though, maybe for the first time, there is one generally applicable design principle that is purely objective when evaluating the quality of a system/module/library/code construct: the hashtag#IndependentVariationPrinciple (IVP).The IVP won't always help y

commenters.name: Joseph Boese
commenters.commentText: I read this and think hmm might be spending the latter half of my career refactoring/rewriting AI created spaghetti code tightly coupled hairballs.  Any decent size code base can be modified by AI until it can't and then good luck meeting business needs without spending money on human coders who wil

commenters.name: Matt E.
commenters.commentText: "Works on my machine" code works also. Should that now be acceptable in production too?  Why not just eliminate all coding standards just because something works?  The time spent writing proper code will be far lower than the time spent debugging, maintaining and ultimately refactoring poorly writte

commenters.name: Nickolai Stepanov
commenters.commentText:
Like "Functional Programming is better Class"...Also, code might be written 1000 ways...Yes maintentable code is not spaghetti code, but almost all subjective, all have pros and cons...AI  makes code review better ))) just need control result.

commenters.commentText: Figures why I'm no senior yet.

commenters.name: Michael S. P. Miller
commenters.commentText: That's why the Systems Aanalyst exists. S/he gathers the requirements and tells the Senior Devs what to code. S/he is the arbiter of correctness.

commenters.name: Kyle Griffin Aretae
commenters.commentText: Indeed...but if its not maintainable 6 months later...which we've seen...then that's the key

"Code works" is merely the most obvious c

AI picks, and does as it's told, and if you tell it the wrong thing, you have screwed yourself in the future. "Wor

commenters.commentText: Code quality is subjective; business value is objective. This is a great reminder to zoom out from the IDE every once in a while and look at the bottom line.

commenters.name: Adi Stan
commenters.commentText: Hi, Peter!Beck talks about the "valley of regret" from an organizational investment angle. I see something else: a fundamental cognitive shift. When a junior works with AI, they are not just compressing search time, they are building their mental models differently. In "Cognitive Divergence Theory "

commenters.name: Maxim Geraskin
commenters.commentText: Peter, could you share your ideas about the requirements for juniors? What should they be able to do? What shouldn't they do?

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: Maxim Geraskin I’m not sure I have an answer right now. commenters.name: Mohammad-Mahdi Khadem-Al-Charieh
commenters.commentText: The biggest challenge of building large projects is figuring out what the customer actually needs, formalizing that in precise language and properly communicating it.That is what the picture is trying to convey.And that is exactly the opposite of what AI is good at.AI will make these issues worse, s

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.commentText: You're touching something real: AI is great at executing what you describe, terrible at questioning whether you described useful thing.AI isn't designed to define what to deliver. That's on us.What if we close that loop? People Ask → AI Ships → measure actual usage, maybe surveys too (though people

commenters.name: Vitalii Honcharenko
commenters.commentText: This is such a clear way to see the shift. AI turns code from a bottleneck into a canvas. The skill is no longer just building, it’s knowing what to build and when to pivot.

commenters.name: Vitalii Honcharenko
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov thanks, true
